subsystem: QPDF PDF object parser. This is QPDFObjectHandle::parseInternal, the core routine that turns a stream of lexical tokens (produced by QPDFTokenizer) into a tree of QPDFObjectHandle values: nulls, booleans, integers, reals, names, strings, operators, indirect references, arrays, and dictionaries. It is the recursive-descent heart of QPDF's PDF-object reader, here rewritten as an explicit iterative state machine driven by three parallel stacks.
provenance: Identified by the fully-qualified method name QPDFObjectHandle::parseInternal(...) and its parameters (PointerHolder<InputSource> input, QPDFTokenizer& tokenizer, StringDecrypter* decrypter, QPDF* context), plus QPDF-specific symbols: qpdf_offset_t, QPDFExc(qpdf_e_damaged_pdf, ...), QTC::TC("qpdf", ...), QUtil::string_to_ll / QUtil::int_to_string, newNull/newBool/newInteger/newReal/newName/newString/newOperator/newIndirect/newArray/newDictionary. These are all part of the QPDF library (Jay Berkenbilt, jberkenbilt/qpdf). The sample banner 'primevul_train_2329_fixed' marks this as the post-fix revision of a QPDF parseInternal change.
trust_boundary: The 'input' InputSource wraps the raw bytes of a PDF file, which is fully attacker-controlled: a malicious or fuzzed PDF can present arbitrary, malformed token streams. Every value returned by tokenizer.readToken(input, object_description) is therefore hostile: token type, token string value, nesting order, and the total count/depth of arrays and dictionaries are all under attacker control. 'decrypter' processes attacker-supplied string bytes, and 'context'/'object_description' are internal. The parser must treat any sequence, depth, and volume of tokens as adversarial and must not crash, overflow, recurse without bound, or consume unbounded resources.

what_the_code_does.explanation: This is the signature of the object parser. 'input' is a reference-counted handle to the byte source of the PDF (attacker data). 'object_description' is a human-readable location label used only for diagnostics. 'tokenizer' is the lexer that yields tokens from 'input'. 'empty' is an out-parameter set true when an 'endobj' is seen with no object read. 'decrypter', when non-null, decrypts PDF string literals in place. 'context' is the owning QPDF (needed to build indirect references) and may be null. 'content_stream' switches the parser into content-stream mode where bare words become operators and EOF is tolerated. The leading comment warns that the routine must not resolve indirect objects here, because resolving them would move the file pointer and corrupt parsing. 'empty' is initialized to false.

This block sets up the explicit-stack state machine that replaces recursion. 'object' holds the most recently built value. 'olist_stack' is a stack of object-lists: each nested array/dictionary gets its own child list of already-parsed elements; it is seeded with one empty list for the top level. 'state_e' enumerates the parser states: st_top (outermost), st_start (just entered a container), st_stop (container just closed), st_eof, st_dictionary, st_array. 'state_stack' tracks the state per nesting level, seeded with st_top. 'offset_stack' records the file offset where each container began (for diagnostics), seeded with the current position input->tell(). 'done' is the loop-termination flag. The three stacks are pushed in lockstep and are always kept the same depth, which is the invariant the pop logic later relies on.

The main loop reads and dispatches one token per iteration. At the top of each pass it snapshots the current frame: 'olist' is a reference to the object-list at the top of olist_stack (the container currently being filled), 'state' is the current parser state, and 'offset' is the current container's start offset. 'object' is reset to an uninitialized handle. Then tokenizer.readToken pulls the next lexical token from the untrusted input. Because .back() is used on all three stacks, the loop depends on those stacks never being empty; the initial seeding at lines 18/21/23 guarantees at least one element, and the pop logic below is guarded to preserve that.

The first switch classifies the token. For end-of-file: in content-stream mode EOF is a legitimate terminator, so the local state is set to st_eof (handled by the second switch). Outside content-stream mode, reaching tt_eof here is considered impossible (the tokenizer would have produced tt_bad and thrown earlier), so it throws std::logic_error as a defensive assertion.

Curly-brace tokens are not valid at object level in PDF, so both open and close braces are handled identically: emit a QTC test-coverage marker, issue a non-fatal warning through warn()/QPDFExc describing the damage, and substitute a null object. This is graceful recovery from malformed input rather than aborting the parse.

Closing tokens are matched against the current state. A ']' while state==st_array transitions the frame to st_stop, signalling the second switch to finalize the array. A '>>' while state==st_dictionary transitions to st_stop for dictionary finalization. A mismatched close token (e.g. ']' seen when not in an array) is treated as damage: it warns and substitutes null instead of underflowing a stack. This state-matching is what prevents a stray close token from popping a frame that was never pushed.

An opening '[' or '<<' begins a new nested container. A fresh empty object-list is pushed onto olist_stack, the current file offset is pushed onto offset_stack, and the new container's state (st_array or st_dictionary, chosen by the token type) is pushed onto state_stack. The local 'state' is set to st_start so the second switch takes no finalizing action this iteration. Note that all three stacks grow by exactly one, keeping them in lockstep. Critically, there is no check here on how deep olist_stack/state_stack have already grown.

These cases build scalar objects directly from the token value. Booleans compare the token string to "true". Nulls need no value. Integers convert the token's C-string via QUtil::string_to_ll; token.getValue().c_str() yields a pointer into the token's own string that is consumed synchronously inside the same full expression, so no dangling occurs. Reals and names wrap the token string. Each simply assigns to 'object', which the second switch then places into the current container.

Bare-word tokens are the most complex case. 'value' binds to the token's string. In content-stream mode any word becomes an operator. Otherwise the parser recognizes the PDF indirect-reference syntax 'obj gen R': it requires state != st_top, at least two elements already in the current list, and that the two trailing elements are non-indirect integers (the object and generation numbers). All indexing uses the bounds-checked .at() and is additionally guarded by olist.size() >= 2, so olist.size() - 1 / - 2 cannot underflow into an out-of-range access. If matched but context is null it throws (cannot build an indirect ref without an owning QPDF); otherwise it builds newIndirect and pops the two consumed integers. A bare 'endobj' at top level with nothing read is treated as an empty object: null is produced, the input is rewound to getLastOffset(), and 'empty' is set true. Any other word is treated as damage and wrapped as a string with a warning.

String literals are copied into a local 'val'; if a decrypter is present it decrypts the bytes in place before the value is wrapped as a new string object. Copying to a local (rather than aliasing the token) avoids mutating the token's storage. The default case is a catch-all for any unexpected token type: warn and substitute null, again preferring recovery over abort.

After the first switch, this is a defensive consistency check: if no object was produced yet the state must be one of the control states (st_start, st_stop, st_eof) that legitimately produce no value; otherwise it throws logic_error. The trailing 'object = newNull();' after the throw is unreachable dead code (execution never returns from throw), but it is harmless.

The second switch acts on the frame state. st_eof: if containers remain open (state_stack.size() > 1) the input ended mid-structure, so it warns; then it stops the loop leaving 'object' uninitialized to signal EOF. st_dictionary/st_array: the just-built 'object' is appended to the current container's list. st_top: a complete top-level object was read, so terminate. st_start: the container was just opened this iteration; nothing to append yet.

st_stop finalizes a container that was just closed. The guard requires both state_stack and olist_stack to hold at least two elements before popping, which prevents underflowing the stacks below their seeded base frame (and, because offset_stack grows in lockstep with olist_stack, protects its later pop too). It captures old_state, pops state_stack, and if the closed container was an array, builds an array object from the accumulated olist.

If the closed container is a dictionary, the flat olist of alternating key/value elements is converted to a std::map. First it collects the set of existing valid name keys. Then it iterates: if an expected key slot holds a non-name or indirect object, it synthesizes a unique '/QPDFFakeN' key (looping next_fake_key until the candidate is not already used), warns, and treats the offending object as the value. If a key is the last element with no following value, it warns and uses null. Otherwise it consumes the next element as the value via olist.at(++i). Each pair is inserted into 'dict', and finally a dictionary object is built. All indexing uses bounds-checked .at() and the i+1 check guards the ++i access, so no out-of-bounds read occurs even for odd-length or malformed key lists.

After finalizing the container, olist_stack and offset_stack are popped (state_stack was already popped at line 248), unwinding one nesting level. If the frame beneath is the top level, parsing is complete and 'done' is set. Otherwise the freshly built array/dictionary object is appended to the parent container's list, continuing the parse of the enclosing structure. When the loop exits, the completed top-level 'object' is returned. This explicit unwinding is exactly what an equivalent recursive parser would do on function return, but without consuming call-stack frames.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: There is no cap on nesting depth. Every '[' or '<<' pushes one frame onto each of the three heap-backed stacks, and nothing bounds how many times this happens. A crafted PDF consisting of a long run of opening tokens (e.g. thousands or millions of '[' or '<<') drives olist_stack/state_stack/offset_stack to grow without limit, consuming heap memory proportional to input length. Converting the historically recursive parser to this iterative form removed the call-stack-exhaustion crash (uncontrolled recursion, CWE-674), but it did not add a depth limit, so the residual failure mode is unbounded memory/CPU consumption (CWE-400) — a denial-of-service on a memory-constrained consumer. The invariant the implementation should uphold is a maximum nesting depth (or total object count) after which parsing is rejected; this shown code enforces none.

Independently of depth, the width of a single container is unbounded: every non-terminating token appends one element to the current olist. A single array containing millions of small elements (e.g. a huge run of integers or nulls) forces the current olist to grow arbitrarily, and when the dictionary branch later runs it additionally builds a std::set of names and a std::map, roughly tripling peak memory for that container. Combined with the unbounded depth above, an attacker gets a strong amplification primitive: a small input file expands into large heap structures. The implementation should bound the aggregate number of parsed objects, not only depth.

This is the classic place an indexing bug would hide: olist.size() is unsigned, so olist.size() - 1 and olist.size() - 2 would underflow to enormous values on a short list. Here it is NOT a bug, because the compound condition short-circuits on olist.size() >= 2 before any subtraction, and because .at() is bounds-checked and would throw rather than read out of bounds even if the guard were wrong. The point to preserve is that this guard must remain paired with the subtractions; if a refactor swaps .at() for operator[] or drops the size check, this becomes an out-of-bounds read (CWE-125). As written it is safe.

This guard is what stops a malicious stream of unmatched close tokens from underflowing the stacks. Note that st_stop is only ever reached when a close token matched the current container state (lines 64/81), and a stray close is instead turned into null (lines 68-77 / 85-94), so an attacker cannot directly force st_stop on the base frame. The guard checks state_stack and olist_stack but not offset_stack; this is safe ONLY because offset_stack is pushed in lockstep with olist_stack (lines 99/101) and both are seeded once (lines 18/23), so their depths are always equal. If a future change pushed offset_stack on a different path, the unguarded offset_stack.pop_back() at line 318 could underflow. The invariant to keep: the three stacks must be mutated strictly in lockstep.

Minor robustness/quality issue, not a memory-safety bug: the statement 'object = newNull();' immediately follows a throw and is therefore unreachable dead code. It has no security impact but indicates the recovery intent (fall back to null) was never actually wired up on this path; if the throw were ever downgraded to a warning the author likely intended to substitute null. Worth noting only so a reader does not assume the fallback executes.

summary: This is the hardened, iterative rewrite of QPDF's QPDFObjectHandle::parseInternal: it parses fully attacker-controlled PDF token streams into an object tree using three lock-step explicit stacks instead of recursion. Memory-safety is well handled — all indexing is bounds-checked .at() guarded by explicit size checks, stray/mismatched tokens are recovered as null rather than underflowing stacks, and the recursion-to-iteration conversion eliminates the prior stack-exhaustion crash. The remaining residual risk is resource exhaustion: neither nesting depth nor total object count is bounded, so a small crafted PDF can expand into large heap structures (DoS).

reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry point; parameters are the trust surface but the signature itself carries no fixed-buffer/length contract to violate.
Simple boolean out-flag; set deterministically, no security contract.
Grows one frame per nested container with no depth cap — unbounded-resource-consumption source.
All six states are handled in the second switch; no missing-case fallthrough.
Pushed/popped in lockstep with olist_stack under the size>=2 guard; cannot underflow. (Growth risk captured under olist_stack finding.)
Popped at line 318 without its own guard, but pushed in lockstep with olist_stack so depth is always >= olist_stack; safe as written.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: while (! done) main loop + .back() snapshots, lines 25-29
reviewed_anchors.reason: .back() on stacks that are always seeded with a base frame; guarded pops preserve non-emptiness.
Unbounded nesting depth — no maximum-depth check before pushing frames.
Mismatched close tokens recover as null instead of popping; prevents stack underflow from stray closes.
Unbounded container width — no per-container or total object-count cap.
c_str() pointer is consumed synchronously within the same full expression; no dangling (CWE-416 does not apply).
size()-1/-2 subtractions are short-circuit-guarded by olist.size()>=2 and use bounds-checked .at(); no OOB/underflow.
Seeks to a previously recorded valid offset; intended rewind, no attacker-controlled arithmetic.
Operates on a local copy of the token bytes; the string transform itself is out of scope of this file.
Unreachable statement after throw — code-quality/latent-intent note, no security impact.
Correctly prevents stack underflow below the base frame before popping.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: dictionary reconstruction loop incl. olist.at(i)/at(++i) and fake-key while-loop (lines 254-315)
reviewed_anchors.reason: All indexing bounds-checked with i+1 guard; fake-key loop terminates against a finite names set; no OOB or infinite loop.
Would require billions of non-name entries to overflow; bounded by container size, impractical and non-memory-unsafe.
Reached only after the size>=2 guard; lockstep depth keeps offset_stack pop safe.

findings.title: No nesting-depth limit in the object parser (unbounded stack growth on deeply nested arrays/dictionaries)
findings.anchor: olist_stack.push_back(...); offset_stack.push_back(...); state_stack.push_back(...) in the tt_array_open/tt_dict_open case
findings.bug_class: Uncontrolled resource consumption via unbounded nesting depth (formerly uncontrolled recursion)
findings.invariant: The parser must enforce a maximum nesting depth (and reject the PDF once exceeded) so attacker-controlled structure depth cannot drive unbounded stack/heap growth.
findings.failure_mode: A crafted PDF containing a long run of opening tokens ('[' or '<<') causes olist_stack/state_stack/offset_stack to grow one frame per token with no cap. The rewrite to an iterative state machine removed the original recursive call-stack exhaustion, but replaced it with unbounded heap growth linear in input length, enabling a memory-exhaustion denial of service on constrained consumers.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether a caller or a member (e.g. a max-depth constant or a check on olist_stack.size()) bounds nesting depth; if none exists, deeply nested input is only limited by available memory.

findings.title: No cap on total object / container-element count
findings.anchor: olist.push_back(object) in the st_dictionary/st_array case of the second switch
findings.bug_class: Uncontrolled resource consumption (unbounded container width / object count)
findings.invariant: The parser should bound the aggregate number of objects it will materialize from a single parse so a small input cannot expand into arbitrarily large heap structures.
findings.failure_mode: A single array or dictionary with a very large number of elements grows the current olist without limit; the dictionary path additionally builds a std::set of names and a std::map, tripling peak memory for that container. Combined with the depth issue this is a small-file-to-large-heap amplification (DoS).
findings.what_to_confirm: Check whether the surrounding QPDF parsing enforces any global object-count or size limit before/while calling parseInternal.

findings.title: Unreachable statement after throw (dead recovery code)
findings.anchor: object = newNull(); following throw std::logic_error(...) in the uninitialized-object consistency check
findings.invariant: Recovery statements intended to run must be reachable; a fallback assignment after an unconditional throw never executes.
findings.failure_mode: No runtime impact: the throw always unwinds, so 'object = newNull();' is never executed. It only signals that a null-fallback recovery was written but never wired up; harmless but misleading.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the intended behavior on this path (throw vs. warn-and-recover); if recovery was intended, the throw should be a warn() and then the null assignment kept.

audit_checklist: Does any caller or member enforce a maximum nesting depth before/while parseInternal pushes onto olist_stack/state_stack/offset_stack? If not, deeply nested PDFs cause unbounded memory growth (DoS).
Is there any bound on the total number of objects or per-container element count materialized during a parse (array width, dictionary size)?
Verify the three stacks (olist_stack, state_stack, offset_stack) are ALWAYS pushed and popped in lockstep on every code path, since the st_stop guard only checks two of them but pops all three.
Confirm all olist indexing stays on bounds-checked .at() with the olist.size()>=2 / i+1 guards intact; a refactor to operator[] or dropping the guard would create an out-of-bounds read (CWE-125).
Confirm QPDFTokenizer::Token::getValue() lifetime so token.getValue().c_str() at line 116 and the const& binding at line 129 cannot dangle across the uses shown.
Verify decrypter->decryptString(val) cannot overflow or over-read the string buffer it mutates (implementation not shown here).
Confirm the fake-key while-loop always terminates (candidate eventually absent from the 'names' set) for adversarial dictionaries full of duplicate/non-name keys.
